worth_it_explanation: An ads.txt file is the public record that tells buyers which sellers a publisher has authorized, and Index Exchange lines in that file are what let Index Exchange bid on the publisher's inventory at all. A missing line, a wrong seller account ID, or a wrong DIRECT or RESELLER designation can cause buyers to discount or skip IX bids, so the file directly affects how much demand reaches the inventory. The check reads a file the publisher already publishes, so it costs review time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a specific line to add, correct, or remove.
intro: An ads.txt file is a plain-text list of Authorized Digital Sellers that a publisher posts at the root of its domain so buyers can confirm which companies are allowed to sell its inventory. For app inventory, the equivalent file is app-ads.txt. This subcategory checks the lines that authorize Index Exchange (IX) specifically: that the correct IX seller account ID is listed with the right DIRECT or RESELLER designation, and that no stale, duplicate, or unauthorized IX lines remain. These checks confirm the file matches the publisher's real selling relationship with IX, so buyers reading it can verify IX bids rather than discount them.

audit_checks.title: The Index Exchange authorization line is missing or carries the wrong account ID or designation
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Buyers use ads.txt to confirm that a bid request for the publisher's inventory comes from an authorized seller. If the Index Exchange line is missing, lists the wrong seller account ID, or carries the wrong DIRECT or RESELLER designation, buyers cannot match the IX bid to an authorized line, so many demand-side platforms filter or discount the inventory. That suppresses demand across IX at scale, which makes this a revenue-level gap rather than a cosmetic one.

educate.base:
An ads.txt file is a public list of Authorized Digital Sellers that a publisher posts at the root of its domain, for example at the path '/ads.txt'. For inventory served inside mobile apps, the equivalent file is app-ads.txt, hosted on the developer domain listed in the app store. Buyers crawl these files and read them before they bid, so the file is the publisher's public statement of which companies, including Index Exchange (IX), are allowed to sell its inventory.

Each line in the file follows a fixed format defined by the IAB Tech Lab specification. The first field is the advertising system's canonical domain, the second is the publisher's seller account ID within that system, and the third is the relationship, either DIRECT or RESELLER. A line authorizing IX needs the IX exchange domain, the seller account ID that IX issued for this publisher, and the relationship that matches how the inventory is actually sold. The seller account ID is significant because buyers match it against the publisher ID that appears in the OpenRTB bid request and against the IX entry in sellers.json, so the value has to be exact.

The DIRECT and RESELLER designation has a specific meaning. DIRECT means the publisher holds the account at IX directly and controls it, which is the case when the publisher integrated IX itself. RESELLER means the publisher has authorized another party, such as a managed-service partner or a parent network, to sell this inventory through its IX account. A line marked DIRECT when the relationship is actually a reseller arrangement, or the reverse, misrepresents the chain and can cause buyers to flag or discount the bid.

When buyers cannot find a matching authorized line, the common result is that they treat the inventory as unauthorized and either filter it out or bid lower on it. The authorization gap can take a few forms:
  • No IX line exists in the file at all, so IX bids have no authorization to match against
  • An IX line exists but the seller account ID does not match the ID IX issued for this publisher
  • An IX line exists but the DIRECT or RESELLER designation does not match the real selling relationship
  • Inventory runs inside apps but app-ads.txt is missing or omits the IX line, even though the web ads.txt includes it
You may want to confirm that the IX authorization line is present and exact across both ads.txt and, where apps are in scope, app-ads.txt. One approach is to pull the seller account ID and relationship that IX assigned for this publisher, then compare them line by line against what the published files actually contain.

site_type_overlays.publishing: A media publisher often sells the same inventory through more than one path, for example directly through its own IX account and also through a network partner that resells. You may want to confirm that each path has its own correctly designated line, a DIRECT line for the publisher's own IX account and a separate RESELLER line for the partner, rather than one line standing in for both.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A commerce-content site that runs ads usually sells through a single direct relationship, so the IX line should typically read DIRECT with the publisher's own seller account ID. You may want to confirm no inherited RESELLER line lingers from a past monetization partner the site no longer works with. Such a line would authorize a seller that should no longer be in the chain.

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's exact seller account ID and the DIRECT or RESELLER designation for this publisher, along with the canonical IX exchange domain to use in the line, are documented in the Index Exchange partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the published ads.txt file, and app-ads.txt where apps are in scope, against the Index Exchange seller account ID and relationship the publisher was issued.

ads_txt_file.label: Published ads.txt and app-ads.txt files
steps: Open the publisher's ads.txt at the root of the domain, for example at 'https://example.com/ads.txt', and read every line that references the Index Exchange exchange domain.
For each Index Exchange line, note the exchange domain, the seller account ID, and the DIRECT or RESELLER designation.
Where app inventory is in scope, open app-ads.txt on the developer domain listed in the app store and repeat the same check for the Index Exchange lines.
Compare each value against the seller account ID and relationship Index Exchange issued for this publisher, recording any line that is missing, has a mismatched ID, or carries the wrong designation.

sellers_json_cross_reference.label: Index Exchange sellers.json
steps: Open the Index Exchange sellers.json file to confirm the seller account ID listed in the publisher's ads.txt resolves to this publisher's entry.
Check that the seller_type in the sellers.json entry (publisher, intermediary, or both) is consistent with the DIRECT or RESELLER designation used in the ads.txt line.
Record any case where the ads.txt seller account ID does not appear in the Index Exchange sellers.json. Buyers cannot verify a line that has no matching seller entry, so note it for follow-up.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for ads.txt and for app-ads.txt where apps are in scope, whether an Index Exchange line is present, whether its seller account ID matches the ID issued for this publisher, and whether its DIRECT or RESELLER designation matches the real selling relationship. A file where the Index Exchange line is present, the seller account ID is exact, and the designation matches passes. A file where the line is missing, the seller account ID is wrong, or the designation is wrong is a fail recorded with the specific line and the value that does not match.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the Index Exchange line is present and whether its seller account ID and DIRECT or RESELLER designation match the issued values above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ads.txt file for {{site_name}} does not carry a correct Index Exchange (IX) authorization line, so the seller account ID is missing, does not match the ID IX issued, or the DIRECT or RESELLER designation does not match the real selling relationship. Buyers read ads.txt to confirm a bid request comes from an authorized seller, and when they cannot match the IX line, many demand-side platforms filter or discount the inventory, which suppresses IX demand across the account. {{voice_recommend}} pulling the exact seller account ID and relationship IX assigned for this publisher from the partner portal, then correcting the line in ads.txt, and in app-ads.txt where apps are in scope, so each value is exact. After the fix, confirm the seller account ID also resolves to this publisher in the IX sellers.json so buyers can verify the chain end to end.

audit_checks.title: Stale, duplicate, or unauthorized Index Exchange lines remain in the file
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Lines left over from past partners or duplicated by repeated edits authorize sellers the publisher may no longer work with, or list the same seller twice, which clutters the supply path and can authorize inventory the publisher did not intend to authorize. That weakens the transparency the file is meant to provide and can expose the publisher to unauthorized reselling, though it usually degrades trust and tidiness rather than cutting demand outright, which keeps it below a revenue-level gap.

educate.base:
An ads.txt file accumulates lines over time. Each time a publisher adds a monetization partner, runs a test, or works with a managed-service vendor, lines get appended, and lines rarely get removed when a relationship ends. The result is a file that can list Index Exchange (IX) sellers the publisher no longer works with, the same IX seller account ID more than once, or a RESELLER line for a partner whose contract has lapsed. Each of those weakens the very authorization signal the file exists to provide.

Stale lines are the most consequential of the three. A RESELLER line left in the file after a partner relationship ended still tells buyers that party is authorized to sell the inventory, so a reseller the publisher has cut ties with can keep transacting against an authorization the publisher meant to revoke. The fix is to remove the line when the relationship ends, which closes the authorization at the same time as the contract.

Duplicate lines are lines that repeat the same IX exchange domain and seller account ID, often introduced when a managed-service partner appends entries the publisher already had, or when a copy-paste during an edit doubles a block. Duplicates do not usually break verification because a buyer only needs one matching line, but they make the file harder to read and audit and can hide a genuine error inside the noise. The IAB Tech Lab specification treats the file as a set of authorized seller records, so a single correct line per seller relationship is the clean state.

Unauthorized lines are IX entries the publisher did not place and cannot account for. These warrant a closer look because an entry the publisher cannot explain may have been added by a vendor with file access or copied from another publisher's file as a template. A few patterns are worth checking for:
  • An IX RESELLER line for a partner the publisher no longer works with
  • The same IX exchange domain and seller account ID appearing on more than one line
  • An IX line the publisher cannot trace to a relationship it set up
  • Leftover IX lines from a past ad-management vendor that still has, or once had, file access
You may want to confirm that every IX line in the file maps to a current, intended selling relationship. One approach is to list each IX line, match it to a known relationship, and remove any line that is stale, duplicated, or unaccounted for.

site_type_overlays.publishing: A media publisher that has worked with several monetization partners over the years is the most likely to carry stale IX RESELLER lines from partners it has since left. You may want to reconcile each IX line against the current partner list and remove any RESELLER entry tied to a relationship that has ended.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A commerce-content site that added ads through a plugin or a third-party ad manager may have had IX lines appended automatically without a clear record of what was added. You may want to confirm each IX line maps to a relationship the site set up on purpose, and remove any line the plugin or vendor left behind that no longer applies.

educate.learn_more_note: The current list of Index Exchange seller account IDs and reseller relationships authorized for this publisher, which is the reference for deciding whether a given line is stale or unauthorized, is available in the Index Exchange partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review every Index Exchange line in the file against the publisher's current, intended selling relationships to find lines that are stale, duplicated, or unaccounted for.

ads_txt_file.label: Published ads.txt and app-ads.txt files
steps: Open the publisher's ads.txt, and app-ads.txt where apps are in scope, and list every line that references the Index Exchange exchange domain.
For each Index Exchange line, record the seller account ID and the DIRECT or RESELLER designation, and group lines that repeat the same exchange domain and seller account ID.
Match each line to a known, current selling relationship, and mark any line that maps to a partner the publisher no longer works with as stale.
Flag any duplicated lines and any Index Exchange line the publisher cannot trace to a relationship it set up.

sellers_json_cross_reference.label: Index Exchange sellers.json
steps: Open the Index Exchange sellers.json and confirm each seller account ID still in the ads.txt resolves to an active entry consistent with the publisher's current relationships.
Note any seller account ID in the ads.txt that no longer has a matching active entry in the Index Exchange sellers.json, which points to a stale line.
Record the lines to remove and the single correct line to keep for each genuine, current relationship.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists every Index Exchange line in the file and matches each to a current, intended selling relationship. A file where every Index Exchange line maps to a current relationship, with no duplicates and no unaccounted-for entries, passes. A file that carries a stale line tied to a partner the publisher no longer works with, a duplicated line, or a line the publisher cannot trace to a relationship it set up is a fail recorded with the specific lines and the reason each one does not belong.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each Index Exchange line and whether it maps to a current selling relationship above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ads.txt file for {{site_name}} carries Index Exchange (IX) lines that are stale, duplicated, or unaccounted for, so the file authorizes sellers the publisher may no longer work with or lists the same seller more than once. Because buyers treat every line in the file as a current authorization, a leftover RESELLER line lets a partner the publisher has cut ties with keep transacting, and duplicates make the file harder to audit. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling each IX line against the publisher's current selling relationships, removing any stale or unaccounted-for line, and collapsing duplicates to a single correct line per relationship. After the cleanup, confirm against the IX sellers.json that every remaining seller account ID still resolves to an active entry for this publisher.
